subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Two Particles on x-axis — Position, Direction & Total Distance
stimulus: A particle $P$ is moving along the $x$-axis. The velocity of particle $P$ at time $t$ is given by $v_P(t) = \sin(t^{1.5})$ for $0 \le t \le \pi$. At time $t = 0$, particle $P$ is at position $x = 5$. A second particle, $Q$, also moves along the $x$-axis. The velocity of particle $Q$ at time $t$ is given by $v_Q(t) = (t - 1.8) \cdot 1.25^t$ for $0 \le t \le \pi$. At time $t = 0$, particle $Q$ is at position $x = 10$.

parts.prompt: Find the positions of particles $P$ and $Q$ at time $t = 1$.
parts.scoring_notes: First point for explicit definite integral. Must earn first point to be eligible for second and third. Adding initial condition to at least one integral earns second point for that particle. Read unlabeled answers left-to-right as $x_P(1)$ then $x_Q(1)$.

scoring_points.description: Presents at least one definite integral

required_elements: Presents at least one definite integral

scoring_points.official_rubric: The first point is earned for the explicit presentation of at least one definite integral, either $\int_0^1 v_P(t)\,dt$ or $\int_0^1 v_Q(t)\,dt$. Writing $\int_0^1 v_P(t) + 5 = 5.371$ (missing $dt$) does not earn a position point, but $5 + \int_0^1 v_P(t) = 5.371$ does. The first point must be earned to be eligible for the second and third points.

sample_responses.commentary: Explicitly presents the definite integral for particle $P$, satisfying the requirement for at least one definite integral with correct limits and integrand.
States only the numerical position without showing any definite integral; a bald calculator answer does not earn the setup point.
Correctly presents the definite integral for particle $Q$ with the initial condition incorporated, earning the first point.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The second point requires adding the initial condition to the integral and finding the correct position. The answer must be correct to three decimal places.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly integrates the velocity, adds the initial position $x = 5$, and reports the answer to three decimal places.
Computes the displacement but forgets to add the initial position $x = 5$; the answer $0.371$ is missing the initial condition.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The third point requires adding the initial position $x_Q(0) = 10$ to the integral of $v_Q$ and reporting the correct value $8.564$. Unlabeled answers are read left to right as $x_P(1)$ then $x_Q(1)$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly sets up the position integral for $Q$, adds the initial position $10$, and obtains the three-decimal answer.
Reports the correct number but the first point was not earned (no definite integral shown), so this position point cannot be earned.

parts.prompt: Are particles $P$ and $Q$ moving toward each other or away from each other at time $t = 1$? Explain your reasoning.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires reference to sign of velocity for at least one particle. Must not present incorrect value for velocity. Second point requires signs of both velocities and relative positions at $t = 1$; references to $t = 0$ positions alone are not sufficient.

scoring_points.description: Uses sign of $v_P(1)$ or $v_Q(1)$ to determine direction

required_elements: Uses sign of $v_P(1)$ or $v_Q(1)$ to determine direction

scoring_points.official_rubric: The first point requires using the sign of $v_P(1)$ or $v_Q(1)$ to determine the direction of motion for one particle. This point cannot be earned without reference to the sign of the velocity. An explicit value for $v_P(1)$ or $v_Q(1)$ is not necessary, but if given it must be correct to up to three decimal places.

sample_responses.response: $v_P(1) = \sin(1) \approx 0.841 > 0$, so particle $P$ is moving to the right.
sample_responses.commentary: Evaluates the velocity at $t = 1$, identifies its sign as positive, and correctly concludes $P$ is moving right.

sample_responses.response: At $t = 1$, particle $P$ is moving to the right and particle $Q$ is moving to the left.
sample_responses.commentary: States directions but provides no reference to the sign of either velocity function — the point cannot be earned without referencing the sign of $v_P(1)$ or $v_Q(1)$.

scoring_points.description: Moving toward each other; at $t = 1$, $x_P(1) \approx 5.371 < x_Q(1) \approx 8.564$, so P is to the left of Q; P moves right and Q moves left, so they move toward each other

required_elements: Moving toward each other; at $t = 1$, $x_P(1) \approx 5.371 < x_Q(1) \approx 8.564$, so P is to the left of Q; P moves right
Q moves left, so they move toward each other

scoring_points.official_rubric: The second point requires an explanation based on the signs of both $v_P(1)$ and $v_Q(1)$ and the relative positions at $t = 1$. References only to $t = 0$ positions are not sufficient.

sample_responses.response: Since $v_P(1) > 0$ and $v_Q(1) < 0$, and $x_P(1) \approx 5.371 < x_Q(1) \approx 8.564$, particle $P$ is to the left of $Q$, $P$ moves right and $Q$ moves left, so they move toward each other.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses both velocity signs, compares relative positions at $t = 1$, and correctly concludes they are moving toward each other.

sample_responses.response: At $t = 0$, $x_P = 5 < x_Q = 10$ so $P$ is left of $Q$. $P$ moves right ($v_P > 0$) and $Q$ moves left ($v_Q < 0$), so they move toward each other.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses the $t = 0$ initial positions rather than the $t = 1$ positions; the relative positions must be evaluated at $t = 1$.

parts.prompt: Find the acceleration of particle $Q$ at time $t = 1$. Is the speed of particle $Q$ increasing or decreasing at time $t = 1$? Explain your reasoning.
parts.scoring_notes: Must explicitly connect $v_Q'(t) = a_Q(t)$. An unsupported value does not earn first point. Second point compares signs of $v_Q(1)$ and $a_Q(1)$; sign of $v_Q(1)$ need not be re-shown if established in part (b).

scoring_points.official_rubric: The acceleration must be explicitly connected to $v_Q'$, e.g., $v_Q'(1) = 1.027$. An unsupported value $1.027$ or $1.026$ without showing $v_Q' = a_Q$ does not earn this point.

sample_responses.commentary: Explicitly connects acceleration to the derivative of velocity and provides the correct three-decimal value.
States the correct value but does not show $v_Q'(1) = a_Q(1)$; the setup connecting $a_Q$ to $v_Q'$ is required.
Uses the correct notation $v_Q'(1)$ for acceleration; the alternate value $1.026$ is acceptable within the rounding tolerance.

scoring_points.description: Speed is decreasing at $t = 1$ because $v_Q(1) < 0$ and $a_Q(1) > 0$ have opposite signs
scoring_points.official_rubric: The second point requires comparing the signs of $a_Q(1)$ and $v_Q(1)$ at $t = 1$. Considering only one sign is not sufficient. The sign of $v_Q(1)$ need not be re-shown if already established in part (b). The second point may be earned without the first, provided no incorrect sign or value of $v_Q(1)$ is presented.

sample_responses.response: $v_Q(1) = -1 < 0$ and $a_Q(1) \approx 1.027 > 0$; the speed is decreasing because velocity and acceleration have opposite signs.
sample_responses.commentary: Compares both signs explicitly and states the correct conclusion — speed decreases when velocity and acceleration have opposite signs.
Only considers the sign of acceleration without comparing it to the sign of velocity; this is insufficient and reaches the wrong conclusion.

parts.prompt: Find the total distance traveled by particle $P$ over the time interval $0 \le t \le \pi$.
parts.scoring_notes: First point for $\int_0^{\pi} |v_P(t)|\, dt$ or equivalent split at zero $t \approx 2.145$. Second point only for correct answer $1.931$; unsupported answer earns no points. A response using $\int_0^{\pi} |v_Q(t)|\, dt$ earns first point but not second.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The first point is earned for $\int_0^\pi |v_P(t)|\,dt$. It may also be earned for a split at the zero $t \approx 2.145$, provided the response indicates $v_P(2.145) = 0$. A response using $\int_0^\pi |v_Q(t)|\,dt$ earns the first point but is not eligible for the second. An unsupported answer of $1.931$ earns no points.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct absolute-value integral for total distance traveled by particle $P$ over $[0,\pi]$.
Uses the signed integral (net displacement) rather than $|v_P(t)|$; this gives displacement, not total distance.
Correct split at the zero of $v_P$ (where $v_P$ changes sign), which is equivalent to the absolute-value integral for total distance.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The second point is earned only for the correct answer $1.931$ and can only be earned after the first point. An unsupported value of $1.931$ earns no points.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct integral setup followed by the correct three-decimal answer.
An unsupported answer without the integral setup earns no points for either part (d).
